_doc: Manual/verified per-problem decisions that override or augment the automatic Phase-1 rules in leetcode_rl_filter.py. Keyed by split -> str(id). action: 'drop' | 'fix_tests' | 'keep'. For fix_tests, 'remove'='failing_best' removes the asserts the model's stored best attempt fails (valid ONLY for verified bad-test problems where every failing assert is confirmed spec-violating and n_failing is small); the generator additionally asserts the canonical passes all REMAINING asserts. Verbose 'reason' + 'evidence' required. All train decisions below verified by tools/leetcode_verify_badtests.py (model-vs-canonical outputs on the failing input).

3337.reason: Constraint-violating tests: all 5 failing asserts pass a nums array of length 25, violating 'nums.length == 26' (one weight per letter a-z). A solution that indexes 26 letters is correct on every in-spec test (107/107) and only fails the 5 malformed 25-length inputs.
3337.evidence: model fails exactly 5 asserts, each with len(nums)==25; passes all 26-length tests.

3312.reason: Systematically constraint-violating tests: 42 of 85 asserts include queries with value >= n*(n-1)/2, violating '0 <= queries[i] < n*(n-1)/2' (there are only that many gcd pairs). Verified the model's solution passes ALL 43 in-spec asserts and fails EXACTLY the 42 out-of-range-query asserts; the canonical also passes all 43 clean. Removing the 42 leaves a sound 43-test suite.
3312.evidence: model-best fails 42/42 OOB-query asserts, 0/43 clean; canonical 0/43 clean.

167.reason: Constraint-violating tests: the 3 failing asserts use all-equal arrays ([0]*10 t=0, [3]*5 t=6, [1]*20 t=2) where MANY index pairs sum to target, violating the stated guarantee 'The tests are generated such that there is exactly one solution.' The model returns a different VALID pair (verified twoSum([0]*10,0)=[1,10], which sums to 0); the test over-strictly demands [1,2].
167.evidence: model fails exactly the 3 multiple-solution asserts; its returned pairs are valid (sum to target).

391.reason: Constraint-violating test: the single failing assert includes degenerate zero-width rectangles [-1,-1,-1,3] and [-1,1,-1,3] (xi==ai), violating '-105 <= xi < ai <= 105' (strict). Exact-cover behavior on zero-area rectangles is undefined.
391.evidence: model->True, canon->False on an input containing xi==ai rectangles.

3025.reason: Constraint-violating tests: both failing asserts repeat a point, violating 'All points[i] are distinct.' (Same family as 939/3027.) With distinct points the model's count is correct; the duplicate enables the canonical's extra degenerate pair.
3025.evidence: both failing asserts contain a duplicated point (has_dup=True).

187.reason: Mis-graded for RL (structural, like 471): the problem says 'You may return the answer in any order', but the grader does order-sensitive exact list-match. The model returns the SAME set of repeated-DNA sequences in a different order and is wrongly failed on 3 asserts. Not fixable by removing a few inputs — the grading method is wrong for the whole suite.
187.evidence: model output == canonical output as a SET, differing only in order (e.g. 'AAAAAAAAAA' placed last vs first).

2905.reason: Mis-graded for RL (structural, like 471): the problem says 'If there are multiple choices for the two indices, return any of them', but the grader exact-matches one canonical pair. The model returns a DIFFERENT VALID pair (verified [4,7] satisfies indexDifference=3 and valueDifference=5). Exact-match is wrong for the whole suite.
2905.evidence: model->[4,7] is a valid answer (|4-7|>=3, |nums[4]-nums[7]|=6>=5); canon->[1,5] also valid.

1960.reason: Buggy ground-truth: the canonical solution (and thus the enshrined expected values) are WRONG. For s='babadabababa' the true max product of two non-intersecting odd-length palindromes is 27 ('babadabab' len 9 x 'aba' len 3), but the GT expects 25; brute force confirms the GT undercounts on 3 inputs. An unreliable reference → not a valid RL target.

421.reason: Constraint-violating tests: failing asserts use nums values up to 3e9, exceeding 'nums[i] <= 2^31-1'. The canonical's 31-bit trie truncates and undercounts the max XOR; the model computes the true (larger) XOR, matching brute force. Model fails exactly the out-of-range asserts; canonical passes all in-spec.

2429.reason: Constraint-violating tests: failing asserts use num values > 10^9, violating '1 <= num1, num2 <= 10^9'. The model handles the larger ints correctly; canonical assumes the bound. Model fails only the out-of-range asserts.

875.reason: Constraint-violating tests: 22 failing asserts have h < piles.length, violating 'piles.length <= h' (infeasible: each pile needs >=1 hour). Expected values are canonical bisect-boundary artifacts. Model fails exactly these 22; passes all feasible inputs.
875.evidence: e.g. len(piles)=10, h=5; model fails 22/22 infeasible, 0 feasible.

1918.reason: Constraint-violating tests: 20 failing asserts use k > n*(n+1)/2 (the number of subarrays), violating '1 <= k <= n*(n+1)/2'. The k-th smallest subarray sum doesn't exist. Model fails exactly these.
1918.evidence: n=5 has 15 subarrays but k=25; model off-by relative to canon's out-of-range extrapolation.

3115.reason: Constraint-violating tests: 3 failing asserts contain zero prime numbers, violating the problem's guarantee that at least one prime exists. A solution that assumes a prime exists IndexErrors; canonical returns None.
3115.evidence: #primes in failing inputs = 0; model IndexError vs canon None.

1983.reason: Constraint-violating tests: failing asserts use nums1/nums2 of UNEQUAL length, violating the stated 'n == nums1.length == nums2.length'. A constraint-faithful solution IndexErrors. Model fails exactly the mismatched-length asserts.
1983.evidence: len(nums1)=35, len(nums2)=34; model IndexError vs canon 34.

287.reason: Constraint-violating tests: 10 failing asserts contain values far outside [1, n] (e.g. 50000 when n=11), violating '1 <= nums[i] <= n' and 'nums.length == n+1'. Floyd's cycle-detection (values-as-indices) IndexErrors on the illegal values. Model fails exactly these.
287.evidence: n=11 but max(nums)=50000; model IndexError vs canon 12.

1904.reason: Constraint-violating tests: 12 failing asserts have loginTime == logoutTime, explicitly violating 'loginTime and logoutTime are not equal.' Model fails exactly these degenerate zero-duration rounds.
1904.evidence: assert numberOfRounds('15:30','15:30')==0; model 96 vs canon 0 on equal times.

153.reason: Constraint-violating tests: 8 failing asserts are NOT valid rotated-sorted-distinct arrays (2+ cyclic descents, or contain duplicates), violating the problem's precondition (sorted ascending then rotated, all unique = LeetCode 153, not 154). Binary-search findMin is undefined on such inputs. Model fails exactly the malformed ones.
153.evidence: e.g. [1,3,2] (2 descents), [3,8,9,1,2,3,...] (duplicate 3); valid input has exactly 1 cyclic descent.

813.reason: Floating-point exact-match fragility: 4 failing asserts differ from the model only in the last ULP (e.g. 18.166666666666668 vs ...664). The model's average is correct to ~15 digits; the gt uses exact float '==' instead of a tolerance. NOTE: the proper fix is tolerance-based float comparison in the grader (would also help other float-return problems); test removal is a stopgap.

688.reason: Floating-point exact-match fragility (same as 813): 10 failing asserts are 1-ULP differences in the knight-probability float (e.g. ...096 vs ...097). Correct to ~15 digits; gt uses exact '=='. Proper fix is tolerance-based grading.

5.reason: Multiple-valid-answer problem mis-graded by exact match (like 471/187): longest palindromic substring has ties (when the max length appears in several places, or length 1 with distinct chars). The model returns a VALID longest palindrome that differs from the canonical's first-found, and is wrongly failed. LeetCode's own judge accepts any; exact-match does not. Structural, not fixable by removing a few inputs.
5.evidence: model 'c' vs canon 'a' (both valid length-1 palindromes when no longer one exists).

229.reason: Unordered-output problem mis-graded by exact match (like 187): majority elements (>n/3) have no defined order. The model returns the SAME set in a different order ([1,2] vs [2,1]) and is wrongly failed. Structural grading defect.

870.reason: Explicit return-any problem mis-graded by exact match (like 471): the statement says 'Return any permutation of nums1 that maximizes its advantage.' The model returns a DIFFERENT permutation achieving the same maximal advantage and is wrongly failed. Structural, not fixable per-input.
870.evidence: model and canon are different permutations with equal advantage count.

1216.reason: Buggy-GT / constraint edge cases: the 2 failing asserts are isValidPalindrome('',0)==False and isValidPalindrome('a',0)==False, but '' and 'a' are already palindromes (0 <= k removals) so the correct answer is True; additionally s='' violates '1 <= s.length'. The canonical mishandles these length-0/1 edges; the model is correct. Model fails exactly these 2.
1216.evidence: 'a' with k=0 is already a palindrome -> True, but gt expects False; s='' violates 1<=s.length.

3137.reason: Constraint-violating tests: 37 failing asserts have k NOT dividing word.length, violating 'integer k such that k divides n'. Model is correct on all divisible (in-spec) inputs and fails exactly the indivisible ones.
3137.evidence: e.g. n=34,k=16; n=36,k=5 (k does not divide n); model fails 37/37 indivisible, 0 divisible.

134.reason: Constraint-violating tests: 52 failing asserts are degenerate (gas==cost, so MULTIPLE start indices complete the circuit), violating 'If there exists a solution, it is guaranteed to be unique.' The model returns a different VALID start; the gt demands one specific index. Model fails exactly the multi-valid asserts.
134.evidence: e.g. gas=[1,1,1,1],cost=[1,1,1,1] -> valid_starts=[0,1,2,3]; model returns a valid start != gt's choice.

224.reason: Majority-contaminated test suite: 91 of 174 asserts contain '*'/'/' (multiplication/division), which Basic Calculator I does NOT support — its charset is 'digits, +, -, (, ), space'. These are Basic-Calculator-II inputs mixed in. Too contaminated to fix by removing a few; and 2 in-spec asserts also fail (separate model bug). Drop.

1850.reason: Substantially contaminated/infeasible test suite: ~30+ failing asserts use num equal to the largest digit-permutation (no greater 'wonderful integer' exists) or k beyond the number of wonderful integers, violating feasibility. Could not cleanly separate the out-of-spec asserts from 2 possible genuine model bugs, so removing-failing-best risks deleting good tests. Drop.
1850.evidence: failing inputs include num='9876543210','321','9999999999' (largest perm => no wonderful integer exists).

1980.reason: Explicit return-any, mis-graded by exact match (like 471): 'If there are multiple answers, you may return any of them' (a binary string of length n not in nums). The model returns a valid absent string differing from the canonical and is wrongly failed. Structural.
1980.evidence: statement: 'you may return any of them'; model output is a valid length-n string not in nums.

1092.reason: Explicit return-any, mis-graded by exact match (like 471): 'If there are multiple valid strings, return any of them' (shortest common supersequence). The model returns a valid SCS of the same length differing from the canonical. Structural.
1092.evidence: statement: 'return any of them'; SCS has many equal-length valid answers.

791.reason: Explicit return-any, mis-graded by exact match (like 471): 'Return any permutation of s that satisfies this property' (custom sort string). Equal characters can be ordered many ways; the model returns a valid permutation differing from the canonical. Structural.
791.evidence: statement: 'Return any permutation of s'; multiple valid orderings of equal chars.

1169.reason: Unordered output, mis-graded by exact match (like 187): 'You may return the answer in any order' (possibly-invalid transactions). The model returns the SAME set in a different order and is wrongly failed. Structural.
1169.evidence: statement: 'return the answer in any order'; model output equals canonical as a set.

336.reason: Unordered output, mis-graded by exact match (like 187): palindrome-pairs returns a list of index pairs with no specified order. The model's set of pairs matches the canonical but in a different order and is wrongly failed. Structural.
336.evidence: model's pair set == canonical's; order differs.

1901.reason: Special-judge problem mis-graded by exact match: 'Find a Peak Element II' accepts ANY peak (multiple valid). The model returns a verified-valid peak differing from the canonical and is wrongly failed. Structural.
1901.evidence: model outputs confirmed to be valid peaks (strictly greater than 4-neighbors).

2664.reason: Special-judge problem mis-graded by exact match: the Knight's Tour has many valid tours. The model produces a verified-legal tour differing from the canonical's DFS order and is wrongly failed. Structural.
2664.evidence: model output is a legal knight's tour (all cells visited once, legal moves).

644.reason: Float-tolerance problem mis-graded by exact match: the statement says 'Any answer with a calculation error less than 10^-5 will be accepted', but the gt uses exact float '=='. The model's max-average is within 1e-5 and is wrongly failed on 65 asserts. The proper fix is tolerance-based grading; structural otherwise.
644.evidence: statement explicitly allows <1e-5 error; gt asserts exact equality.

1515.reason: Float-tolerance problem mis-graded by exact match: 'Answers within 10^-5 of the actual value will be accepted', but the gt uses exact float '=='. The model's optimum is within 1e-5 and is wrongly failed on 93 asserts. Proper fix is tolerance-based grading; structural otherwise.
1515.evidence: statement explicitly allows <1e-5 error; gt asserts exact equality.

2311.reason: Constraint-violating test: 1 assert feeds k=2147483647 (=2^31-1), exceeding the stated bound k<=10^9. A solution coded to the real bound is correct returning 31 (31 ones form value 2^31-1 <= k); the test wrongly demands 30.
2311.evidence: assert #85 longestSubsequence('1'*48, k=2147483647): model->31 (correct), canon->30 (capped to the 10^9 bound).

939.reason: Constraint-violating test: point [10,10] appears twice, violating 'All the given points are unique.' A real area-25 rectangle exists among the unique points; the test expects 0 (an artifact of the canonical choking on the duplicate y-value).

3175.reason: Constraint-violating tests: two asserts use repeated skill values, violating 'All integers in skills are unique.' The first game becomes a tie (skill 1 vs skill 1), whose outcome the spec never defines; model and canonical pick opposite winners only because the tie is unspecified.
3175.evidence: asserts #87,#92 findWinningPlayer with repeated 1s: model->1, canon->0 (incumbent-wins-tie); no tie can occur under the stated uniqueness constraint.

3027.reason: Constraint-violating tests: two asserts repeat a point (e.g. [5,5]), violating 'All points[i] are distinct.' With distinct points the answer is 24; the extra pair counted by the canonical is the degenerate coincident-point placement that only the duplicate enables.
3027.evidence: asserts #85,#86 numberOfPairs with a duplicated point: model->24 (correct on distinct points), canon->25.

1094.reason: Constraint-violating test: trips use to=2000..5000, violating '0 <= fromi < toi <= 1000'. A solution that sizes its difference array to the stated max (1001) correctly IndexErrors on the illegal input.
1094.evidence: assert #43 carPooling(... to=5000 ...): model->IndexError (array sized to constraint), canon->True.

1711.reason: Constraint-violating test: deliciousness values reach ~2^28, violating '0 <= deliciousness[i] <= 2^20'. A solution iterating powers of two up to 2^21 (correct for the stated bound) undercounts on the out-of-range input.
1711.evidence: assert #44 countPairs(... values ~2^28 ...): model->2 (correct for 2^20 bound), canon->9.

3026.reason: Constraint-violating test: uses k=0, violating '1 <= k'. For k=0 a single element (|first-last|=0) is a valid good subarray giving -1; a solution coded for k>=1 forms only multi-element pairs and returns -2.

3237.reason: Constraint-violating test: a query names window 26 while n=25, violating '1 <= queries[i] <= n'. Behavior on an out-of-range window is undefined; canonical surfaces a phantom window, model ignores it.
3237.evidence: assert #22 simulationResult(n=25 windows, query 26): model omits the phantom 26, canon prepends 26.

1643.reason: Constraint-violating test: destination=[3,7], k=123, but total paths = nCr(10,3)=120, violating 'k <= nCr(row+column, row)'. There is no 123rd path; the lexicographic k-th is undefined.
1643.evidence: assert #34 kthSmallestPath([3,7], k=123): model->'VVVVVVHHHH', canon->'VVVVVVVVVV' (out-of-range k).

1439.reason: Constraint-violating test: k=100 while n^m = 3^4-style cap min(200, n^m)=81, violating 'k <= min(200, n*m...)'. The 100th smallest sum does not exist.
1439.evidence: assert #49 kthSmallest(4x3 mat, k=100): model->IndexError, canon->17000 (k beyond the number of sums).

852.reason: Constraint-violating test: the array has multiple local peaks, violating 'arr is guaranteed to be a mountain array.' Binary peak search is only defined on a true (single-peak) mountain.
852.evidence: assert #21 peakIndexInMountainArray(multi-peak arr): model->32, canon->38 (non-mountain input).

1620.reason: Constraint-violating test: a tower has coords (60,70), violating '0 <= xi, yi, qi <= 50'. The spec bounds only tower coordinates, not the returned coordinate; the out-of-range tower drives the divergence (and arguably the true network-quality optimum lies outside the canonical's implicit 0..50 grid).

1256.reason: Underspecified edge: the encoding is defined by a TABLE rendered as an image that is absent from the prompt text. The model recovers the intended encoding from the surviving examples and matches the canonical on 137/138 tests; the only divergence is the genuinely-ambiguous num=0 boundary ('0' vs ''). Removing that single edge assert leaves a cleanly-determined, solvable problem.
1256.evidence: assert #11 encode(num=0): model->'0', canon->'' (no num=0 example or table to disambiguate).

471.reason: Fundamentally mis-graded for RL: the problem explicitly says 'If there are several solutions, return any of them' (Example 3 lists multiple valid encodings), but the grader does exact string-match against one canonical output. The model returns a valid optimal-length encoding ('a9[a]', 5 chars) that the test rejects in favor of '10[a]'. The defect is the grading METHOD, not specific inputs, so it is not fixable by removing a few asserts; keeping it would penalize correct, creative outputs and inject reward noise.
471.evidence: assert #13 encode('a'*10): model->'a9[a]' (valid, len 5), canon->'10[a]' (len 5). Both are correct under 'return any'.

1066.reason: NOT a bad test (reviewed). The flagged input is fully in-spec (n=m=10, unique locations). The model's attempt enumerates permutations(range(m), n) (~3.6M iters) and times out on the full suite; the canonical is efficient. This is a legitimate failure requiring an efficient algorithm — genuine RL headroom, not a test/spec defect.
1066.evidence: assert #87 assignBikes(9 workers/10 bikes): model->TimeoutError (brute-force permutations), canon->fast DP/Hungarian.
